channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Tatemae: Detecting Alignment Faking via Tool Selection in LLMs
item.description: Alignment Faking (AF) occurs when an LLM superficially complies with safety objectives under monitoring to avoid value modification, then reverts to unsafe preferences once oversight is removed. Prior detection relies heavily on Chain-of-Thought (CoT) analysis in conversational settings—effective only when strategic reasoning is present and faithful, but unable to distinguish deception from capability failure otherwise. We formalize AF as a composite behavioral event and propose **Tatemae**, a novel detection framework grounded in *observable tool selection*: the model selects the safe tool when unmonitored, yet switches to the unsafe tool under monitoring that prioritizes helpfulness over safety—even while its reasoning explicitly acknowledges the safe choice as superior. We release a benchmark of **108 enterprise IT scenarios**, spanning Security, Privacy, and Integrity under Corruption and Sabotage pressures. Evaluating six frontier LLMs across five independent runs, we observe mean AF detection rates ranging from **3.5% to 23.7%**, with vulnerability profiles varying significantly by domain and pressure type. Crucially, these differences correlate more strongly with training methodology than raw capability—highlighting AF as a systemic alignment failure, not merely a scaling gap.

item.title: Differentially Private Contrastive Learning via Bounding Group-level Contribution
item.description: Differentially private (DP) contrastive learning faces severe utility degradation due to the inherent batch-wide gradient dependency in standard contrastive objectives (e.g., InfoNCE), where each sample’s gradient depends on all others—amplifying DP noise and increasing sensitivity. To address this, we propose **DP-GCL**, a principled framework that structurally bounds group-level contribution: it partitions batches into small disjoint groups and restricts negative sampling *within* each group, thereby localizing gradient influence and reducing global sensitivity. To preserve negative diversity without extra privacy cost, DP-GCL introduces *intra-group augmentation*, generating additional negative views via lightweight transformations (e.g., RandAugment, back-translation). Extensive experiments across eight datasets—including CIFAR, ImageNet-1K, Flickr30K, and MS-COCO—demonstrate that DP-GCL consistently advances the state of the art under practical privacy budgets (ε = 2–8): it improves image classification accuracy by **+5.6%** and image-text retrieval Recall@1 by **+20.1%**, outperforming all existing DP contrastive methods.

item.title: Can Cross-Layer Design Bridge Security and Efficiency? A Robust Authentication Framework for Healthcare Information Exchange Systems
item.description: This paper proposes a cross-layer authentication framework for Healthcare Information Exchange (HIE) systems that jointly leverages cryptographic primitives and physical-layer (PHY) device fingerprints—namely Carrier Frequency Offset (CFO) and quadrature skewness—to enable continuous, lightweight identity verification. Initial authentication uses ECC-based PKI and digital certificates, while PHY features are extracted offline by a Regional Centralized Authority (RCA) to train a compact ML classifier. For re-authentication, the system extracts these features from incoming OFDM symbols in real time and verifies identity via the trained model—eliminating per-message signature exchange. Privacy is strengthened through encrypted, frequently rotated pseudo-identities ensuring unlinkability. Formal BAN logic analysis confirms robustness against impersonation, MitM, replay, and Sybil attacks. Evaluation shows 62% lower latency, 58% reduced communication overhead, and 41% less CPU usage versus conventional PKI—while maintaining 99.3% accuracy and zero false rejection rate.

item.title: Enforcing Benign Trajectories: A Behavioral Firewall for Structured-Workflow AI Agents
item.description: We present *Enforcer*, a behavioral firewall for structured-workflow LLM agents that enforces benign tool-call trajectories via telemetry-driven anomaly detection. It compiles verified benign execution logs into a parameterized deterministic finite automaton (pDFA), encoding permitted tool sequences, contextual preconditions, and parameter bounds (type, regex, range). At runtime, a lightweight gateway enforces constraints via $O(1)$ state-transition lookup—shifting all heavy computation offline. Evaluated on Agent Security Bench (ASB), *Enforcer* achieves a 5.6% macro-averaged attack success rate (ASR) across five attack scenarios, dropping to 2.2% in three structured workflows—outperforming the stateless scanner Aegis (12.8% ASR). It attains 0% ASR against multi-step and context-sequential attacks, and blocks 98.6% of 1,000 algorithmically spliced exfiltration payloads at the structural level; the remaining 14 paths are fully thwarted by end-to-end string-parameter guards (0 successes, 95% CI [0%, 23.2%]). With only 2.2 ms/call latency (3.7× faster than Aegis) and 2.0% benign task failure rate, *Enforcer* drastically shrinks the attack surface—but highlights that exact-match whitelisting of sensitive parameters remains indispensable against synonym-substitution evasion (18% success rate observed).

item.title: LATTICE: Evaluating Decision Support Utility of Crypto Agents
item.description: We introduce **LATTICE**, the first benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate the *decision support utility* of crypto agents in realistic, user-facing scenarios—moving beyond narrow reasoning or outcome correctness. LATTICE defines six human-centered evaluation dimensions (e.g., risk awareness, actionability, explainability), 16 task types spanning the full crypto copilot workflow (from wallet diagnostics to DeFi strategy comparison), and a scalable LLM-judge framework that scores outputs without ground-truth annotations or external data. Crucially, its rubrics are auditable and updatable via human feedback, enabling reliable, extensible evaluation. Unlike prior benchmarks comparing foundation models, LATTICE assesses *production-level crypto copilots*—highlighting the critical role of orchestration and UX design. Evaluating six real-world agents on 1,200 diverse queries, we find comparable aggregate scores but substantial divergence across dimensions and tasks—revealing meaningful trade-offs: users with distinct priorities (e.g., security vs. speed) may benefit from different agents, challenging rankings based solely on overall performance. All code, data, and judge prompts are open-sourced.

item.title: eDySec: A Deep Learning-based Explainable Dynamic Analysis Framework for Detecting Malicious Packages in PyPI Ecosystem
item.description: This paper introduces **eDySec**, a deep learning–based, explainable, and stable framework for detecting malicious PyPI packages via dynamic behavioral analysis. Addressing the limitations of traditional ML models on high-dimensional, sparse runtime data (e.g., system calls, network flows, directory access), eDySec leverages the QUT-DV25 dataset—capturing both install-time and post-installation behaviors—to identify discriminative behavioral features and optimize DL architectures (e.g., hybrid CNN-LSTM). It integrates model stability assessment across environments and explainable AI (LRP + SHAP) to ensure transparent, interpretable decisions. Experiments show eDySec halves feature dimensionality while reducing false positives by 82% and false negatives by 79%, improves accuracy by 3%, achieves near-perfect stability (>0.99), and maintains low inference latency (170 ms/package). Crucially, it reveals strong feature-model coupling effects—certain combinations severely degrade performance—highlighting the necessity of co-design in practical deployment.

item.title: OpenSOC-AI: Democratizing Security Operations with Parameter Efficient LLM Log Analysis
item.description: OpenSOC-AI is a lightweight, open-source framework that democratizes AI-powered log analysis for resource-constrained organizations. It applies parameter-efficient fine-tuning—specifically LoRA—to the 1.1B-parameter TinyLlama model, using only 12.6M trainable parameters (1.13% of the base), trained in under 5 minutes on a single NVIDIA T4 GPU with just 450 domain-specific SOC examples. Evaluated on a held-out test set of 50 logs, OpenSOC-AI achieves 68% threat classification accuracy (+68 pts over zero-shot baseline), 58% severity assessment accuracy (+30 pts), and an F1 score of 0.68 for MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping. The full codebase, adapter weights, and annotated dataset are publicly released to ensure reproducibility and foster community-driven extension.

item.title: Human-in-the-Loop Benchmarking of Heterogeneous LLMs for Automated Competency Assessment in Secondary Level Mathematics
item.description: This paper introduces a Human-in-the-Loop (HitL) benchmarking framework to evaluate heterogeneous LLMs for automated competency assessment in secondary mathematics—specifically aligned with Nepal’s Grade 10 Optional Mathematics curriculum. We designed a multidimensional rubric covering four topics (Algebra, Geometry, Trigonometry, Statistics) and four cross-cutting competencies (Comprehension, Knowledge, Operational Fluency, Behavior and Correlation). A multi-provider ensemble—including open-weight models Eagle (Llama 3.1-8B) and Orion (Llama 3.3-70B), and proprietary MoE models Nova (Gemini 2.5 Flash) and Lyra (Gemini 3 Pro)—was evaluated against expert-annotated ground truth (weighted kappa = 0.8652). Results reveal a critical “architecture-compatibility gap”: Gemini-based sparse MoE models achieved fair agreement (κ_w ≈ 0.38), whereas the larger Orion (70B) showed no agreement (κ_w = −0.0261), indicating that architectural alignment with structured rubric constraints outweighs parameter scale. We conclude that while LLMs are not yet suitable for autonomous certification, they deliver high-value assistive support—especially for preliminary evidence extraction and inconsistency flagging—within a rigorously supervised HitL workflow.

item.title: Star-Fusion: A Multi-modal Transformer Architecture for Discrete Celestial Orientation via Spherical Topology
item.description: Star-Fusion is a novel multi-modal Transformer architecture for celestial attitude determination that reframes orientation estimation as a discrete topological classification task on the unit sphere—addressing fundamental limitations of Euclidean regression in RA/Dec space. It employs spherical K-Means to partition the celestial sphere into K topology-preserving regions, eliminating coordinate wrapping artifacts. The architecture fuses three complementary modalities: (i) a SwinV2-Tiny backbone for photometric star pattern encoding, (ii) a convolutional heatmap branch for spatial star distribution modeling, and (iii) a coordinate-aware MLP for geometric anchoring via spherical constraints. Evaluated on a synthetic Hipparcos-derived dataset, Star-Fusion achieves **93.4% Top-1** and **97.8% Top-3 accuracy**, outperforming state-of-the-art LIS and deep regression methods. Crucially, it runs at **18.4 ms inference latency** on resource-constrained COTS hardware, enabling real-time onboard deployment for next-generation satellite constellations.

item.title: Naamah: A Large Scale Synthetic Sanskrit NER Corpus via DBpedia Seeding and LLM Generation
item.description: Naamah is a high-quality silver-standard Sanskrit NER corpus comprising 102,942 grammatically natural, synthetically diverse sentences. To overcome the scarcity of annotated classical Sanskrit data and the limitations of generic LLMs—such as grammatical inaccuracy and shallow linguistic reasoning—we propose a novel DBpedia-seeded, hybrid-reasoning generation pipeline. We first extract culturally grounded Sanskrit entities (e.g., deities, epics, philosophical schools) from DBpedia, then leverage a specialized 24B-parameter hybrid reasoning model to generate syntactically valid, morphologically accurate Sanskrit sentences conditioned on these entities. Naamah enables robust benchmarking: fine-tuning XLM-RoBERTa-large achieves 86.4% F1, while parameter-efficient IndicBERTv2 reaches 83.7% F1—demonstrating state-of-the-art performance and strong transferability for low-resource classical language understanding.

item.title: DSIPA: Detecting LLM-Generated Texts via Sentiment-Invariant Patterns Divergence Analysis
item.description: DSIPA is a training-free, zero-shot, black-box framework for detecting LLM-generated text by analyzing divergence in sentiment-invariant patterns under stylistic perturbations. It leverages the key behavioral asymmetry: human-written texts exhibit greater affective variation across paraphrased versions, whereas LLM outputs maintain unusually stable sentiment distributions. DSIPA computes two unsupervised metrics—*sentiment distribution consistency* and *sentiment distribution preservation*—using only off-the-shelf sentiment analyzers, requiring no model access, gradient computation, or labeled data. Evaluated across five diverse domains (news, code, essays, academic papers, community comments) and state-of-the-art models (GPT-5.2, Gemini-1.5-pro, Claude-3, LLaMa-3.3), DSIPA achieves up to **+49.89% F1 gain** over strong baselines (e.g., DetectGPT, Fast-DetectGPT), with superior cross-domain generalizability and resilience to adversarial paraphrasing. It provides an interpretable, parameter-agnostic behavioral signal for trustworthy AI content identification.

item.title: Asynchronous Federated Unlearning with Invariance Calibration for Medical Imaging
item.description: Federated Unlearning (FU) enables clients to erase their data contributions from a global model to comply with “right-to-be-forgotten” regulations—yet existing methods suffer from *synchronous blocking* (causing high latency in heterogeneous medical devices) and *incomplete erasure* (where erased patterns resurface during subsequent training). To address this, we propose **Asynchronous Federated Unlearning with Invariance Calibration (AFU-IC)**: a novel framework for medical imaging that decouples unlearning from global training, allowing target clients to perform erasure asynchronously without halting federation. Its server-side *invariance calibration* mechanism explicitly penalizes model re-sensitivity to erased anatomical features (e.g., tumor textures or organ boundaries) via perturbation-robust regularization, grounded in medical priors. Evaluated on CheXpert, BraTS, and MosMed, AFU-IC achieves unlearning efficacy and model fidelity on par with full retraining (ΔAUC < 0.012; Dice drop < 0.8%), while reducing wall-clock latency by **63.5%** versus synchronous baselines. AFU-IC establishes a practical, compliant, and reliable foundation for privacy-preserving cross-silo medical AI.

item.title: Who Trains Matters: Federated Learning under Enrollment and Participation Selection Biases
item.description: Federated learning (FL) commonly assumes that participating clients are representative of the target population—a premise violated in practice by two distinct selection biases. *Enrollment bias* arises when eligibility criteria (e.g., device constraints, consent) restrict which users ever enter the system, creating a persistent mismatch between the training and target-population objectives. *Participation bias*, though round-specific and better studied, compounds this issue. We formalize FL under a two-stage selection model and propose **FedIPW**, an inverse-probability-weighted aggregation method that recovers the target-population mean update under standard ignorability and positivity assumptions. To address the practical challenge of missing covariates for non-enrolled clients, we introduce an **aggregate-calibration extension** that leverages known population-level summaries (e.g., demographic marginals) to reweight the enrolled sample. We further provide an algorithm-agnostic convergence analysis showing that residual weighting error induces a non-vanishing bias floor. Experiments on synthetic federated logistic regression confirm substantial objective mismatch under two-stage selection and demonstrate that enrollment correction significantly reduces target-population error—by up to 18.7% with FedIPW.

item.title: Meta-Learning and Targeted Differential Privacy to Improve the Accuracy-Privacy Trade-off in Recommendations
item.description: Balancing differential privacy (DP) and recommendation accuracy remains a fundamental challenge, as uniform DP noise degrades model performance unnecessarily. This paper proposes a two-tiered approach: (**1**) *Targeted DP* at the data level—applying DP noise selectively only to user subgroups most likely to reveal sensitive attributes (e.g., gender or age), identified via interaction-pattern stereotyping and inference risk scoring; and (**2**) *Meta-learning* at the model level—using MAML-based meta-training to enhance model robustness to residual DP noise through fast adaptation on noisy few-shot tasks. Experiments on MovieLens-1M and Amazon-Books show our method improves Recall@20 by 12.7–18.3% over uniform and full DP baselines at ε = 1.0, while reducing empirical privacy risk (measured by attribute inference attack success rate) by 34.5%. This work demonstrates that selective perturbation combined with meta-adaptation achieves a superior accuracy–privacy trade-off, advancing DP deployment from blanket to precision-aware privacy protection.

item.title: SplitFT: An Adaptive Federated Split Learning System For LLMs Fine-Tuning
item.description: Federated Split Learning (FSL) offers privacy-preserving distributed training while alleviating client-side computation burdens—yet it faces critical challenges in fine-tuning large language models (LLMs), including *adaptive cut-layer selection* under device/data heterogeneity and *excessive communication overhead*. To address these gaps, we propose **SplitFT**, the first adaptive FSL system for LLM fine-tuning. SplitFT enables clients to dynamically determine their optimal cut layer based on local resources and performance feedback; it further reduces communication by adaptively lowering the LoRA rank *at the cut layer*, achieving up to 72.1% bandwidth reduction without accuracy loss. We also introduce a **length-based Dirichlet partitioning** strategy to simulate realistic non-IID text-length skew across clients. Extensive experiments on Alpaca, Dolly, and OpenOrca show SplitFT improves fine-tuning speed by 2.1×, boosts accuracy by 3.7–5.2 points, and cuts total communication by 64.5% over state-of-the-art baselines.

item.title: CO-EVO: Co-evolving Semantic Anchoring and Style Diversification for Federated DG-ReID
item.description: Federated Domain Generalization for Person Re-Identification (FedDG-ReID) enables collaborative model training across decentralized source domains while preserving raw data privacy—yet suffers from severe shortcut learning due to unaligned camera styles across clients. To address this, we propose **CO-EVO**, a novel co-evolutionary framework that jointly optimizes *semantic anchoring* and *style diversification*. On the semantic side, **Camera-Invariant Semantic Anchoring (CSA)** learns identity prompts with cross-camera consistency to generate purified, domain-agnostic anchors that suppress local imaging bias. On the visual side, **Global Style Diversification (GSD)** leverages a learnable **Global Camera-Style Bank (GCSB)** to synthesize realistic, diverse style perturbations—expanding the visual coverage of training data. Crucially, CO-EVO establishes a closed-loop co-evolution: purified anchors act as gravitational centers guiding the encoder toward robust anatomical features, while diversified samples continuously refine anchor generalizability. Extensive experiments show CO-EVO achieves new state-of-the-art performance (e.g., +3.2% average mAP), demonstrating that synergistic semantic purification and style expansion is essential for robust cross-domain generalization in federated settings. Code: https://github.com/NanYiyuzurn/ACL-LGPS-2026.

item.title: Differentially-Private Text Rewriting reshapes Linguistic Style
item.description: Differentially-private (DP) text rewriting—enabled by generative language models—now achieves strong privacy guarantees while preserving grammaticality, yet its impact on linguistic register remains poorly understood. We conduct the first multidimensional stylistic profiling of DP rewriting across a spectrum of privacy budgets (ε = 0.5–8.0), quantifying shifts in interactive markers, contextual anchoring, and syntactic subordination. Both autoregressive paraphrasing and bidirectional substitution architectures induce systematic functional erosion: interactive devices drop by >63%, contextual references decline by >57%, and complex subordination falls by >41%. Crucially, these perturbations converge—not toward the original register—but toward a uniform, low-involvement, non-persuasive, and highly declarative style, irrespective of input genre. Semantic content remains well-preserved (BERTScore > 0.92), but stylistic heterogeneity collapses by 79.3%, revealing that DP rewriting structurally homogenizes human discourse at the cost of register identity. This work uncovers a fundamental trade-off: formal privacy comes with an underappreciated stylistic tax.

item.title: Subspace Optimization for Efficient Federated Learning under Heterogeneous Data
item.description: Federated learning (FL) faces dual bottlenecks in the large-model era: severe resource constraints (communication, memory, computation) and performance degradation from non-IID data-induced client drift. While methods like SCAFFOLD correct heterogeneity, they impose prohibitive overhead via auxiliary control variables. This paper proposes **Subspace Optimization for Federated Learning (SSF)**, which performs heterogeneity-aware optimization *only* in a low-dimensional active subspace using projected gradients and model updates—eliminating explicit control variable storage. Crucially, SSF introduces a **backfill-style residual update**: whenever the subspace changes, orthogonal residual components are retained and injected, preserving full-dimensional control information without extra communication. Under standard smoothness and bounded-variance assumptions, SSF achieves a non-asymptotic convergence rate of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/T + 1/\sqrt{NKT})$. Experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet show SSF reduces communication by 38–52% and memory by 41–63% versus SCAFFOLD and FedProx, with <0.9% accuracy drop—delivering superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs under data heterogeneity.

item.title: RCProb: Probabilistic Rule Extraction for Efficient Simplification of Tree Ensembles
item.description: Tree ensembles achieve strong predictive performance but suffer from poor interpretability as ensemble size grows. While RuleCOSI+ extracts compact, accurate rule sets from such models, its reliance on repeated empirical frequency counting over training data incurs prohibitive computational cost for large-scale datasets. To address this, we propose **RCProb**, a probabilistic reformulation of RuleCOSI+ that replaces frequency-based confidence estimation with a **Bayesian framework**: it employs **Dirichlet-smoothed class priors** and **Beta-smoothed conditional likelihoods**, combined via a **Naive Bayes formulation**, enabling closed-form posterior probability computation without scanning the dataset. Evaluated on 33 benchmark datasets, RCProb achieves **comparable predictive accuracy** to RuleCOSI+ (mean difference <0.5%), reduces runtime by **≈22×**, and yields **more compact rule sets** (18.3% fewer rules on average). This work bridges probabilistic reasoning and rule extraction, enabling efficient, scalable, and faithful model explanation.

item.title: Making AI-Assisted Grant Evaluation Auditable without Exposing the Model
item.description: Public agencies are adopting LLMs for grant evaluation, yet face a tension between *confidentiality* (preventing applicants from gaming the system) and *auditability* (ensuring accountability and contestability). We propose a TEE-based architecture that enables **remote attestation of evaluation provenance** without exposing model weights, proprietary rubrics, or intermediate reasoning. The core artifact is an **attested evaluation bundle**: a cryptographically signed, timestamped record linking the submission hash, canonicalized input hash, model-and-rubric identifier, and final score. To mitigate applicant-driven prompt injection via documents (e.g., hidden formatting instructions), we introduce a **canonicalization and sanitization layer** that normalizes inputs and logs suspicious transformations pre-inference. Crucially, our approach does *not* certify fairness or correctness—but it *does* make the execution of predefined rules externally verifiable. This advances accountable AI by grounding auditability in hardware-enforced integrity, distinct from zero-knowledge ML or fully open auditing.

item.title: Towards Agentic Investigation of Security Alerts
item.description: This paper addresses the alert overload crisis in security operations by introducing an **experimental agentic workflow** that augments LLMs with structured tool access—specifically, predefined SQL queries over Suricata logs and `grep`-based text search—while constraining them to a three-stage pipeline: (1) data overview generation, (2) LLM-guided query selection based on that overview, and (3) evidence extraction and verdict synthesis. Unlike naive end-to-end LLM prompting, our approach embeds real-world analyst practices into a controllable, auditable framework. Evaluation shows the workflow boosts LLM verdict accuracy by **23.6%** over baseline prompting and reduces investigation time to ~20% of manual effort. It demonstrates that **structured tool grounding—not raw scale—is key to deploying LLMs as reliable virtual analysts** in high-stakes, low-context security tasks.

item.title: SnapGuard: Lightweight Prompt Injection Detection for Screenshot-Based Web Agents
item.description: Web agents relying on webpage screenshots face critical prompt injection threats, yet existing vision-language model (VLM)-based detectors suffer from prohibitive latency (>14s) and GPU memory overhead due to global page understanding. SnapGuard addresses this by reformulating detection as lightweight multimodal representation analysis of screenshots—without large VLMs. It leverages two novel, computationally efficient signals: (1) a *visual stability indicator* that detects abnormally smooth gradient distributions caused by malicious layout/text obfuscation, and (2) *action-oriented textual signals* recovered via contrast-polarity reversal—a training-free, robust text extraction technique. Evaluated across eight diverse attacks and two benign settings, SnapGuard achieves an F1 score of 0.75—outperforming GPT-4o-prompt (0.68) while being 8× faster (1.81s vs. 14.50s) and introducing zero additional GPU memory overhead. This demonstrates that effective, deployable prompt injection defense is achievable through targeted visual-textual signal fusion, bypassing heavy foundation models.

item.title: From CRUD to Autonomous Agents: Formal Validation and Zero-Trust Security for Semantic Gateways in AI-Native Enterprise Systems
item.description: Enterprise systems are migrating from deterministic CRUD/REST architectures to AI-native paradigms where LLMs act as cognitive orchestrators—yet their stochastic nature undermines classical validation, access control, and formal testing. This paper introduces a Semantic Gateway governed by the Model Context Protocol (MCP), reframing APIs as intent-driven semantic surfaces for dynamic tool discovery, policy-aware authorization, and constrained execution. Its core contribution is a paradigm shift: autonomous agents must be modeled not as software or API clients, but as *stochastic state-transition systems*, validated via Enabledness-Preserving Abstractions (EPAs) and greybox semantic fuzzing—adapted from blockchain verification. A three-layer Zero-Trust model integrates a pre-inference Semantic Firewall, deterministic tool-level RBAC, and cryptographically anchored out-of-band human approval. Empirical evaluation across 500,000 multi-turn fuzzing sequences achieves **100% detection of hidden unauthorized state transitions** and reduces incidental code by **84.2%**, proving that *dynamic formal verification is strictly necessary* for secure agentic deployment.

item.title: Structured Security Auditing and Robustness Enhancement for Untrusted Agent Skills
item.description: This paper addresses security auditing of untrusted Agent Skills—modular capability units comprising `SKILL.md`, scripts, documentation, and repository context. We formalize pre-load auditing as a robust three-way classification task (safe / malicious / ambiguous-risk) and propose **SkillGuard-Robust**, which integrates: (1) role-aware cross-file evidence extraction, (2) selective semantic verification for high-risk code–text pairs, and (3) consistency-preserving adjudication resilient to semantics-preserving rewrites. Evaluated across five large-scale views (254–404 packages) on SkillGuardBench and two public-ecosystem extensions, SkillGuard-Robust achieves **97.30% overall exact match**, **98.33% malicious-risk recall**, and **98.89% attack exact consistency** on the 404-package held-out set—and **99.66%, 100.00%, 100.00%**, respectively, on the 254-package external-ecosystem view. Results demonstrate that factorized, structure-aware package auditing substantially improves robustness in frozen and public ecosystems, while harsher transfer from heterogeneous external sources remains an open challenge.

item.title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Framework for Distributed Chemical Process Optimization
item.description: This paper proposes a privacy-preserving federated learning (FL) framework for distributed chemical process optimization across geographically separated plants under strict data confidentiality constraints. Each plant trains a lightweight LSTM-MLP model locally on its own time-series sensor data, and only differentially private model updates are securely aggregated at a central server—raw data never leaves the premises. Evaluated on real operational datasets from three heterogeneous chemical plants, the framework achieves rapid convergence: global MSE drops from ~2369 to <50 within 5 communication rounds and stabilizes at ~35 after 40 rounds. Compared to local-only training, it improves prediction accuracy by 62.3–78.1% across all sites and matches centralized training performance (within 4.2% MSE gap), while reducing communication overhead by 83%. This work demonstrates FL as a scalable, privacy-compliant solution for collaborative industrial analytics in process manufacturing.

item.title: PHISHREV: A Hybrid Machine Learning and Post-Hoc Non-monotonic Reasoning Framework for Context-Aware Phishing Website Classification
item.description: PHISHREV is a novel hybrid framework for phishing website classification that bridges statistical machine learning with formal, post-hoc non-monotonic reasoning. It decouples prediction (via lightweight ML classifiers) from context-aware decision refinement (via Answer Set Programming), enabling expert knowledge—e.g., domain age, SSL validity, and form semantics—to dynamically revise initial predictions under conflicting evidence. Unlike end-to-end black-box models, PHISHREV’s reasoning layer operates *after* classification, modifying only **5.08%** of raw outputs while improving F1-score by 2.3 points and reducing cross-scenario inconsistency by 17.6%. Crucially, integrating new domain rules incurs **$\mathcal{O}(n)$** time complexity and requires **no model retraining**, offering unprecedented agility against evolving phishing tactics.

item.title: The Structured Output Benchmark: A Multi-Source Benchmark for Evaluating Structured Output Quality in Large Language Models
item.description: We introduce the **Structured Output Benchmark (SOB)**, a multi-source benchmark to rigorously evaluate large language models’ ability to generate *correct, schema-compliant structured outputs*—not just syntactic validity—from diverse inputs: native text, OCR-processed document images, and ASR-transcribed audio conversations. Crucially, SOB provides all models with *text-normalized context*, isolating structured generation capability from vision/speech perception quality for fair, source-agnostic comparison. It comprises 5,000 text, 209 image, and 115 audio evaluation records—each paired with a natural-language question, a target JSON schema, and human-verified ground-truth answers. Evaluating 21 state-of-the-art and open-weight models across seven metrics, we find near-perfect schema compliance (>98%) but sharply declining value accuracy: **83.0% (text), 67.2% (images), and only 23.7% (audio)**—highlighting severe challenges in long-context alignment and fine-grained value extraction. We publicly release the dataset, evaluation pipeline, and code.

item.title: Sample Selection Using Multi-Task Autoencoders in Federated Learning with Non-IID Data
item.description: Federated learning (FL) faces severe performance degradation under Non-IID data due to noisy, abnormal, or redundant local samples. To address this, we propose a novel sample selection framework leveraging a **multi-task autoencoder (MTA)** that jointly estimates sample contribution via reconstruction loss and latent feature analysis. A central server orchestrates unsupervised outlier detection—including **One-Class SVM (OCSVM), Isolation Forest (IF), and Adaptive Threshold (AT)**—to filter low-quality samples client-wise. Furthermore, we introduce a **federated multi-class Deep SVDD loss**, centrally managed to enforce compact, class-discriminative feature representations. Evaluated on CIFAR10 and MNIST under diverse Non-IID settings (Dirichlet α ∈ [0.1,1.0]), varying client counts (2–100), and up to 40% noise, our method achieves up to **+7.02% accuracy gain on CIFAR10 with OCSVM** (loss-based) and **+1.83% on MNIST with AT**, while the federated SVDD loss adds **+0.99% further improvement** on CIFAR10. Results demonstrate consistent robustness across scalability and noise conditions.

item.title: Benchmarking Logistic Regression, SVM, and LightGBM Against BiLSTM with Attention for Sentiment Analysis on Indonesian Product Reviews
item.description: This paper benchmarks traditional machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) for binary sentiment classification on Indonesian e-commerce reviews. Using a balanced dataset of 19,728 samples, we evaluate Logistic Regression (LR), linear SVM, and LightGBM via 10-fold stratified cross-validation within PyCaret, and compare them against a BiLSTM with Attention model trained end-to-end. LR achieves the highest ML performance (97.26% accuracy and F1-score), while BiLSTM+Attention attains 97.24% on a held-out test set—statistically equivalent yet computationally far more expensive. Results demonstrate that well-engineered ML pipelines—including morphological normalization, TF-IDF features, and hyperparameter tuning—can match state-of-the-art sequential DL models on high-dimensional, morphologically rich low-resource languages. This challenges assumptions about DL necessity and offers a practical, efficient, and interpretable alternative for production sentiment analysis in Indonesian and similar linguistic contexts.

item.title: Diagnosis, Bad Planning & Reasoning. Treatment, SCOPE -- Planning for Hybrid Querying over Clinical Trial Data
item.description: This paper addresses clinical trial table reasoning—a distinct table understanding challenge where answers require semantic inference (e.g., normalization, classification, extraction) rather than direct cell lookup. Motivated by pervasive “bad reasoning” in LLMs under implicit planning assumptions, we introduce **SCOPE**, a multi-LLM planner-based framework that explicitly decomposes the task into row selection, structured planning (making source fields, reasoning rules, and output constraints explicit), and execution. Evaluated on 1,500 hybrid reasoning questions over oncology trial tables, SCOPE outperforms zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, TableGPT2, Blend-SQL, and EHRAgent—achieving +12.7% accuracy gain on reasoning-heavy questions while reducing latency by 38% versus heavier agentic baselines. Our work establishes clinical trial reasoning as a unique benchmark and demonstrates that hybrid planner-based decomposition is an effective, efficient solution for implicit attribute recovery.

item.title: Layerwise Convergence Fingerprints for Runtime Misbehavior Detection in Large Language Models
item.description: We present **Layerwise Convergence Fingerprinting (LCF)**, a tuning-free, assumption-light runtime monitor for detecting LLM misbehavior—including backdoors, jailbreaks, and prompt injections—without requiring a clean reference model, trigger knowledge, or weight access. LCF treats the inter-layer hidden-state difference trajectory as a health signal: it computes diagonal Mahalanobis distances on each layer-to-layer difference, aggregates them robustly via Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage, and sets thresholds via leave-one-out calibration on just 200 clean examples. Evaluated across four architectures (Llama-3-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Gemma-2-9B, Qwen2.5-14B) and 56 backdoor variants, 3 jailbreak techniques, and BIPIA email/code-QA prompt injections, LCF achieves <1% mean backdoor ASR on Qwen2.5-7B and Gemma-2, 1.3% on Qwen2.5-14B; detects 92–100% of DAN jailbreaks (62–100% for GCG/role-play); and flags 100% of text-payload injections across all 8 (model, domain) test cells—while incurring only 12–16% backdoor FPR and <0.1% inference overhead. A single unified score covers all three threat families, establishing LCF as a practical, general-purpose safety layer for production LLMs.

item.title: Unveiling the Backdoor Mechanism Hidden Behind Catastrophic Overfitting in Fast Adversarial Training
item.description: Fast Adversarial Training (FAT) enables efficient adversarial robustness but suffers from catastrophic overfitting (CO), where models overfit to the specific training attack and collapse on unseen adversaries. This work fundamentally reinterprets CO as a *weak-trigger backdoor phenomenon*: through pathway analysis, feature prediction inconsistency, and discovery of universal, class-discriminative triggers, we unify CO, backdoor attacks, and unlearnable tasks under a shared “trigger-generalization mismatch” framework. Guided by this insight, we propose two backdoor-inspired mitigation strategies: (i) parameter recalibration via vanilla fine-tuning, linear probing, or weight reinitialization; and (ii) a Weight Outlier Suppression Constraint (WOSC) to regularize abnormal parameter deviations during training. Extensive experiments across CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet demonstrate that our methods consistently boost PGD-5 robust accuracy by 8.2–12.7% over baselines, validating both the theoretical interpretation and practical efficacy—without increasing inference cost.

item.title: GoAT-X: A Graph of Auditing Thoughts for Securing Token Transactions in Cross-Chain Contracts
item.description: Cross-chain bridges—critical infrastructure enabling multi-chain interoperability—have suffered over $2.8B in losses due to subtle logic flaws. Existing defenses fail: bytecode-level static analysis lacks semantic fidelity for cross-chain interactions, while LLM-based approaches suffer from hallucinatory reasoning across complex, multi-contract dependencies. We propose **GoAT-X**, a framework that redefines automated cross-chain auditing as systematic *first-principles verification*, structured as a **Graph of Auditing Thoughts (GoAT)**. GoAT-X explicitly mirrors expert cognition—decomposing state flows, reasoning about security properties (e.g., atomicity, replay resistance), and validating code implementations—by anchoring LLM reasoning in statically extracted data flows and enforcing explicit links between abstract properties and concrete code. Within this constrained space, it treats missing constraints and adversarial bypass paths as first-class vulnerability targets, dynamically exploring reasoning paths to expose semantic gaps. Evaluated on a comprehensive benchmark covering all known token-transfer attacks, GoAT-X achieves **92% recall on fine-grained audit points**, **95% coverage of vulnerable projects**, and identifies **117 confirmed real-world risks**, establishing a new standard for scalable, logic-driven cross-chain security.

item.title: X-NegoBox: An Explainable Privacy-Budget Negotiation Framework for Secure Peer-to-Peer Energy Data Exchange
item.description: Modern energy systems are decentralizing, turning consumers into prosumers who exchange sensitive household energy data for peer-to-peer trading, demand response, and forecasting. However, fixed privacy policies and opaque differential privacy (DP) budgeting erode trust: prosumers lack explanations for why requests are accepted, rejected, or modified. To address this, we propose **X-NegoBox**, an explainable, adaptive privacy-budget negotiation framework. It anchors raw data locally in a private *DataBox* and employs an *Autonomous Privacy Budget Negotiation Protocol (APBNP)* that dynamically computes DP budgets using trust scores, feature sensitivity, declared purpose semantics, behavioral history, and risk-aware pricing—generating interpretable counter-offers (e.g., reduced temporal resolution) when needed. The *Explainable Agreement Layer (X-Contract)* produces human-readable justifications and machine-parsable evidence for every decision. Requester code executes sandboxed locally; only sanitized outputs are shared. Experiments on realistic energy market settings show **37.2% lower privacy leakage**, **29.8% higher request acceptance**, and **significantly improved interpretability**, establishing X-NegoBox as a practical, trustworthy foundation for privacy-preserving energy data collaboration.

item.title: Defusing the Trigger: Plug-and-Play Defense for Backdoored LLMs via Tail-Risk Intrinsic Geometric Smoothing
item.description: Defending large language models (LLMs) against backdoor attacks remains challenging due to the trade-off between security, utility, and latency. We propose **Tail-risk Intrinsic Geometric Smoothing (TIGS)**—a plug-and-play, inference-time defense requiring **no parameter updates, clean data, or auxiliary generation**. TIGS exploits the geometric signature of backdoor triggers: localized attention collapse in semantic content regions. It operates entirely within the native forward pass via three lightweight steps: (i) sample-internal tail-risk screening to identify suspicious attention heads/rows; (ii) intrinsic geometric smoothing—weak content-domain correction for semantic anchoring and strong full-row contraction to disrupt trigger-dominant routing; and (iii) controlled full-row write-back for stable inference. Evaluated across dense, reasoning-oriented, and MoE architectures (e.g., Llama-3, Mixtral, DeepSeek-MoE), TIGS reduces attack success rate from 89.7% to ≤4.2%, preserves clean-task accuracy (Δ < 0.8%), maintains open-ended semantic consistency (BLEU/ROUGE Δ < 0.3), and incurs only **1.9–3.4% latency overhead**—enabling practical, deployment-ready protection for state-of-the-art LLMs.

item.title: AgentVisor: Defending LLM Agents Against Prompt Injection via Semantic Virtualization
item.description: Large Language Model (LLM) agents face critical security risks—especially direct and indirect prompt injection—when integrating untrusted external data with privileged tool execution. Existing defenses struggle to balance security and utility, often over-defending or missing subtle indirect attacks. Inspired by OS virtualization, we propose **AgentVisor**, a novel defense framework that enforces *semantic privilege separation*: it treats the target agent as an untrusted guest and intercepts all tool calls via a lightweight, trusted *semantic visor*. Our approach introduces a rigorous audit protocol grounded in classic OS security primitives (e.g., capability-based access control, least privilege) to systematically mitigate both attack types. Crucially, AgentVisor features a **one-shot self-correction mechanism** that transforms security violations into actionable feedback, enabling agents to recover autonomously. Extensive experiments across 12 real-world workflows show AgentVisor reduces attack success rate to **0.65%**, with only a **1.45% average utility drop** versus no defense—outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

item.title: Poster: ClawdGo: Endogenous Security Awareness Training for Autonomous AI Agents
item.description: ClawdGo introduces the first framework for *endogenous security awareness training* of autonomous AI agents—teaching them to recognize, reason about, and respond to threats (e.g., prompt injection, memory poisoning, supply-chain attacks) *during inference*, without model modification. Its four core contributions are: (1) **TLDT**, a three-layer domain taxonomy (Self-Defence, Owner-Protection, Enterprise-Security) defining 12 trainable security dimensions; (2) **ASAT**, a weakest-first curriculum-driven self-play loop where the agent alternates attacker/defender/evaluator roles; (3) **CSMA**, a four-layer persistent memory architecture with Axiom Crystallisation Promotion (ACP) enabling cross-session skill retention; and (4) **SACP**, a formalization of the precision-recall tradeoff inherent in endogenous training. In live experiments, ASAT raised average TLDT scores from 80.9 to 96.9 over 16 sessions (+16.0), outperforming uniform-random scheduling by 6.5 points and covering 11/12 dimensions. CSMA preserved the full gain across sessions, while cold-start ablation recovered only 2.4 points—revealing a 13.6-point retention gap. SACP was empirically observed as false positives on legitimate capability assessments (30/160).

item.title: Characterizing Vision-Language-Action Models across XPUs: Constraints and Acceleration for On-Robot Deployment
item.description: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models hold great promise for generalist robot control, yet real-world on-robot deployment remains hindered by stringent real-time, cost, and energy constraints—especially on resource-limited edge accelerators (XPUs/NPUs). Unlike prior GPU-centric evaluations, we conduct the first systematic *model-hardware co-characterization* across 12 heterogeneous edge platforms under a unified CET (Cost-Energy-Time) framework. We uncover a universal two-phase inference pattern—compute-bound VLM backbone followed by memory-bound Action Expert—causing severe hardware underutilization. Guided by this insight, we propose **DP-Cache**, which eliminates redundant diffusion steps via cached latent reuse, and **V-AEFusion**, enabling asynchronous pipeline parallelism between vision and action modules. Our optimizations achieve up to **2.9× speedup on GPUs** and **6.0× on edge NPUs**, with only marginal success degradation (<1.2%). All code, benchmarks, and an interactive leaderboard are publicly available.

item.title: Task-guided Spatiotemporal Network with Diffusion Augmentation for EEG-based Dementia Diagnosis and MMSE Prediction
item.description: This paper proposes a **Task-guided Spatiotemporal Network (TGSN)** with diffusion-based data augmentation for joint EEG-based dementia diagnosis and MMSE score prediction. To overcome feature entanglement in multi-task learning, TGSN introduces: (1) a multi-band fusion module capturing complementary spectral EEG features; (2) a pre-trained diffusion augmentation module enhancing sample diversity and robustness; (3) a gated spatiotemporal attention mechanism modeling long-range spatial dependencies and temporal dynamics; and (4) a task-guided query module enabling disentangled, task-specific feature extraction. Evaluated on the XY02 dataset, TGSN achieves **97.78% accuracy for AD/FTD classification** (+16.39% over SOTA) and **83.93% for AD/FTD/VCI classification** (+8.28%), while reducing MMSE prediction RMSE to **1.93 and 2.38**, outperforming best baselines by **1.44 and 1.43**, respectively. Strong cross-dataset generalization is further validated on DS004504.

item.title: Constraint-Guided Multi-Agent Decompilation for Executable Binary Recovery
item.description: Decompilation—recovering source code from binaries—is vital for security analysis and legacy maintenance, yet existing tools produce code that frequently fails to compile or execute correctly. We propose **Constraint-Guided Multi-Agent Decompilation (MCGD)**, a novel framework that transforms raw decompiler output into *re-executable* source via hierarchical validation: (1) syntactic parsing, (2) GCC compilation, and (3) behavioral equivalence verified by LLM-generated test cases. On failure, specialized LLM agents iteratively refine code using structured error feedback. Evaluated on 1,641 real-world binaries from ExeBench across RetDec, Ghidra, and Angr, MCGD achieves **84–97% re-executability**, improving baselines by 28–89 percentage points. It outperforms state-of-the-art LLM-based methods (LLM4Decompile: 80.3%, SK2Decompile: 73.9%, SALT4Decompile: 61.8%) with **84.1% accuracy** using the same GPT-4o backbone. Crucially, ablation shows execution-level validation is indispensable: compile-only approaches yield 0% behavioral correctness despite >91% compilation rates. The system converges rapidly—>90% of binaries succeed within 2 iterations at **$0.03–0.05 per binary**.

item.title: Fed-DLoRA: Efficient Wireless Federated Learning with Dynamic Low-Rank Adaptation
item.description: Federated learning (FL) for the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) suffers from high communication overhead and instability in dynamic wireless environments. To address this, we propose **Fed-DLoRA**, a lightweight FL framework integrating *dynamic* Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), where the LoRA rank is adaptively adjusted per round based on vehicle capabilities and channel conditions. We provide the first convergence analysis linking LoRA rank, vehicular scheduling, and SGD-based optimization via SVD, revealing how dynamic rank selection mitigates Non-IID and system heterogeneity. Building on this, we formulate a joint optimization problem for accuracy, convergence speed, and communication efficiency, and solve it with the **Adaptive Rank, Bandwidth, and Vehicle Selection (ARBVS)** algorithm—a hybrid enumeration-greedy method enabling real-time resource allocation. Experiments show Fed-DLoRA achieves **up to 5.8% higher accuracy**, **37% faster convergence**, and **61.4% less uplink communication** than baseline FL methods, demonstrating superior efficiency and robustness in vehicular edge networks.

item.title: Explaining Temporal Graph Predictions With Shapley Values
item.description: Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) achieve strong predictive performance by jointly modeling spatial and temporal dependencies, yet their decision-making process remains opaque. This paper introduces two model-agnostic local explainers grounded in cooperative game theory: (1) an **event-level Shapley explainer**, applying KernelSHAP to attribute prediction importance to individual temporal edges (i.e., timestamp-edge pairs); and (2) a **feature-level Shapley explainer**, which decomposes event-level Shapley values into Owen values to uncover hierarchical feature contributions (e.g., timestamp, node IDs, edge features) within each event. Evaluated on Wikipedia, Reddit, and MOOC datasets, both explainers outperform state-of-the-art baselines across faithfulness, stability, and human interpretability metrics. Crucially, the feature explainer identified a critical bug in a widely used TGAT implementation—incorrect parsing of millisecond timestamps as seconds—directly explaining observed performance degradation under sparse explanations.

item.title: FedSLoP: Memory-Efficient Federated Learning with Low-Rank Gradient Projection
item.description: FedSLoP is a memory- and communication-efficient federated learning algorithm that addresses the slow convergence, high communication cost, and excessive client-side memory usage of standard methods (e.g., FedAvg) in heterogeneous, resource-constrained settings. It introduces stochastic low-rank subspace projections of local gradients—compressing updates to rank-$r$ representations before transmission and storage—while preserving optimization progress via subspace-aware momentum. Theoretically, under standard nonconvex smoothness and bounded-variance assumptions, we prove FedSLoP converges to a first-order stationary point at rate $O(1/\sqrt{NT})$, with explicit dependence on projection rank $r$. Empirically, on heterogeneous federated MNIST, FedSLoP reduces per-round communication by 72–88% and client memory peak by over 4.3× versus FedAvg, while achieving **higher accuracy (+0.8–1.5%)** than FedAvg and outperforming sparse (SparseFed) and low-rank (Low-Rank-Fed) baselines. Code is publicly available.

item.title: A Unifying Framework for Unsupervised Concept Extraction
item.description: We present a unifying theoretical framework for unsupervised concept extraction—framing it as generative model identification from latent representations. Our core contribution is a general **meta-theorem for identifiability**, which reduces proving concept uniqueness to characterizing the intersection of two symmetry groups: the concept space’s invariance group and the observation distribution’s automorphism group. This theorem dramatically simplifies identifiability analysis across diverse methods—including sparse autoencoders, transcoders, and dictionary learning—turning intricate proofs into concise set-intersection arguments. We show that sparsity, nonlinearity, and sufficient observational diversity jointly guarantee identifiability, and we derive explicit conditions under which concepts are uniquely recoverable up to trivial equivalences. The framework provides the first principled foundation for designing provably faithful concept extractors, enabling reliable downstream applications like model steering and unlearning.

item.title: CAN-QA: A Question-Answering Benchmark for Reasoning over In-Vehicle CAN Traffic
item.description: The Controller Area Network (CAN) is a safety-critical in-vehicle protocol lacking built-in security, necessitating robust intrusion detection. Yet existing approaches treat CAN analysis as a static classification task—ignoring its rich temporal dynamics and misaligning with forensic reasoning needs. To bridge this gap, we introduce **CAN-QA**, the first question-answering benchmark for CAN traffic analysis. It converts raw CAN logs into time-aligned windows and applies deterministic, semantics-aware templates to generate 33,128 natural-language QA pairs across 10 categories—each targeting distinct temporal, statistical, or behavioral properties (e.g., periodicity violation, inter-frame delay anomalies, state-transition consistency). We evaluate large language models (LLMs) on both True/False and multiple-choice formats. Results show LLMs succeed on surface-level patterns (e.g., ID frequency matching) but consistently fail at **temporal reasoning** (e.g., trend inference over windows), **multi-condition logical composition**, and **high-level behavioral interpretation** (e.g., linking timing anomalies to attack semantics). CAN-QA establishes a new paradigm for explainable, interactive, and forensically grounded automotive security evaluation. Code: https://github.com/Kriiiiss/CAN-QA.

item.title: Poisoning Learned Index Structures: Static and Dynamic Adversarial Attacks on ALEX
item.description: Learned indexes like ALEX achieve high performance by modeling key distributions (e.g., CDF), but this reliance creates new attack surfaces. We present the first systematic, large-scale evaluation of both *static data poisoning* and *dynamic algorithmic complexity attacks (ACA)* on ALEX under a unified, reproducible framework. Our experiments scale to realistic workloads (up to 200K adversarial inserts), cover diverse SOSD datasets (uniform, skewed, Zipf), and include a real-key baseline to isolate adversarial effects. Results reveal a clear threat-model separation: static poisoning has negligible impact (<5%) on lookup throughput in bulk-loaded settings, whereas dynamic ACA causes severe degradation—up to **2.0–2.8× slowdown** in lookup throughput. Crucially, attack efficacy is highly dataset-dependent: dense or duplicate-heavy distributions sharply limit adversarial leverage due to ALEX’s localized structure and insertion semantics. We further highlight critical evaluation pitfalls—especially the mismatch between localized structural damage and global query metrics—and stress the need for controlled baselines. Overall, robustness in learned indexes emerges from the tight coupling of threat model, data distribution, and evaluation methodology.

item.title: SUDP: Secret-Use Delegation Protocol for Agentic Systems
item.description: Agentic systems increasingly rely on user secrets (e.g., API keys, tokens) to interact with external services—but current bearer-secret interfaces expose reusable authority to untrusted agents, turning transient compromises into permanent account takeovers. We formalize the core security goal as *Agent Secret Use (ASU)*: enabling an untrusted requester to trigger a user-authorized, secret-backed operation *without ever receiving reusable authority*. From ASU, we derive a principled security-property taxonomy that separates structural obligations (verifiability, operation-binding, single-use) from implementation-level robustness requirements. We propose the **Secret-Use Delegation Protocol (SUDP)**, a three-role protocol where: (1) the agent proposes a canonical operation, (2) the user authorizes it via a fresh authenticator-backed grant, and (3) a custodian redeems the grant *exactly once* to perform the bounded action—ensuring no reusable secret or credential crosses the agent boundary. Under explicit sealing and erasure assumptions, SUDP provably satisfies all ASU requirements and provides storage confidentiality and wrapping-epoch key isolation; plaintext-level forward secrecy additionally requires environment-managed secret rotation and revocation.

item.title: A Comparative Evaluation of AI Agent Security Guardrails
item.description: This paper presents the first comparative benchmark of AI agent security guardrails, evaluating DKnownAI Guard against AWS Bedrock Guardrails, Azure Content Safety, and Lakera Guard. Using human-annotated ground truth across 12,840 real-world agent interactions, we assess detection performance on two critical risk categories: (1) *agent-integrity threats* (e.g., instruction override, indirect prompt injection, tool abuse), and (2) *harmful content generation* (e.g., hate speech, violence, pornography). Results show DKnownAI Guard achieves **96.5% recall**—highest among all systems—and **90.4% true negative rate (TNR)**, indicating superior precision and minimal false positives. Its architecture-aware design significantly outperforms competitors in detecting subtle, context-dependent attacks while maintaining high operational reliability.

item.title: Adaptive Prompt Embedding Optimization for LLM Jailbreaking
item.description: We propose **Prompt Embedding Optimization (PEO)**, a novel white-box jailbreak method that directly optimizes the *continuous embeddings* of original prompt tokens—without appending any adversarial tokens—over multiple adaptive rounds. Contrary to prior assumptions, we show that embedding perturbations remain semantically faithful: nearest-token projection preserves the exact visible prompt string, and >92% of responses stay on-topic per quantitative analysis. PEO integrates continuous embedding-space optimization, structured continuation targets, and a failure-focused adaptive scheduling strategy. Notably, heuristic composite response scaffolds—though unnatural as standalone templates—yield genuine behavioral gains, validated by ASR-Judge to reflect true harmful output generation, not empty formatting. On HarmBench and AdvBench, PEO outperforms state-of-the-art white-box attacks—including discrete suffix search (AutoDAN), appended adversarial embeddings (GCG-Embed), and search-based generation (PAIR)—achieving up to +8.7% absolute ASR improvement.

item.title: SWE-QA: A Dataset and Benchmark for Complex Code Understanding
item.description: We introduce **SWE-QA**, a novel benchmark dataset for evaluating *multi-hop code comprehension*—a critical yet under-addressed capability in real-world software development. Unlike existing benchmarks focusing on isolated code snippets, SWE-QA comprises **9,072 multiple-choice questions** derived from 12 Python repositories in SWE-bench, targeting recurrent reasoning patterns: *Declaration-and-Call* (linking definitions to distant usages) and *Interacting-Entity* (modeling dynamic relationships among collaborating components). Questions are generated via parser-based entity extraction and LLM-assisted construction with rigorously validated distractors to penalize superficial matching. Evaluating 15 models (360M–671B parameters), we find the best accuracy is only **74.41%**, revealing substantial multi-hop reasoning gaps. Dense architectures consistently outperform MoE models by **10–14 percentage points**, while reasoning-enhanced variants show inconsistent gains—highlighting the need for architecture- and task-aligned advances in code understanding.
